title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Suppose I run a website with a homepage prompt encouraging visitors to sign up for a newsletter. I want to know which wording works better. One version says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and another says “Stay up to date by signing up!”
To answer this question, I conduct an A/B test where visitors are randomly assigned to one of the two versions. I then compare the sign-up rates across groups. Because the assignment is random, any difference in outcomes can be attributed to the wording of the call to action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Suppose I run a website with a homepage prompt encouraging visitors to sign up for a newsletter. I want to know which wording works better. One version says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and another says “Stay up to date by signing up!”
To answer this question, I conduct an A/B test where visitors are randomly assigned to one of the two versions. I then compare the sign-up rates across groups. Because the assignment is random, any difference in outcomes can be attributed to the wording of the call to action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or does not sign up, so the outcome is binary. This makes the Bernoulli model a natural framework for the data. Let \(\pi_A\) be the probability that a visitor signs up after seeing CTA A, and let \(\pi_B\) be the probability for CTA B.
The parameter of interest is the difference in sign-up rates:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
\]
In practice, the true value of \(\theta\) is unknown, so we estimate it using the difference in sample means:
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
\]
Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean corresponds to the observed conversion rate. ## Simulating Data
To better understand the behavior of statistical estimators, I simulate an A/B test. I assume that the true sign-up rate for CTA A is 0.22 and for CTA B is 0.18.

set.seed(42)

pi_A <- 0.22
pi_B <- 0.18
n <- 1000

A <- rbinom(n, 1, pi_A)
B <- rbinom(n, 1, pi_B)

theta_true <- pi_A - pi_B
theta_hat <- mean(A) - mean(B)

data.frame(
  Group = c("CTA A", "CTA B"),
  Sample_Rate = c(mean(A), mean(B))
)

  Group Sample_Rate
1 CTA A       0.213
2 CTA B       0.186

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers states that as the sample size increases, the sample average converges to the population mean.

diffs <- A - B
cum_avg <- cumsum(diffs) / seq_along(diffs)

plot(
  cum_avg,
  type = "l",
  lwd = 2,
  xlab = "Number of observations",
  ylab = "Cumulative average of A - B",
  main = "Law of Large Numbers"
)


abline(h = theta_true, lty = 2)








The plot shows that the estimate becomes more stable and approaches the true value as the sample size increases.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
To measure uncertainty, I use the bootstrap method, which repeatedly resamples the observed data.

B_boot <- 1000
boot_thetas <- numeric(B_boot)

for (i in 1:B_boot) {
  A_star <- sample(A, size = n, replace = TRUE)
  B_star <- sample(B, size = n, replace = TRUE)
  boot_thetas[i] <- mean(A_star) - mean(B_star)
}

boot_se <- sd(boot_thetas)

pA_hat <- mean(A)
pB_hat <- mean(B)

analytic_se <- sqrt((pA_hat * (1 - pA_hat)) / n + 
                    (pB_hat * (1 - pB_hat)) / n)

ci_lower <- theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper <- theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

data.frame(
  Metric = c("Estimate", "Bootstrap SE", "Analytical SE", "CI Lower", "CI Upper"),
  Value = c(theta_hat, boot_se, analytic_se, ci_lower, ci_upper)
)

         Metric        Value
1      Estimate  0.027000000
2  Bootstrap SE  0.017680682
3 Analytical SE  0.017861551
4      CI Lower -0.007654136
5      CI Upper  0.061654136

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem states that the sampling distribution of the estimator is approximately normal when the sample size is large.

hist(
  boot_thetas,
  breaks = 30,
  main = "Sampling Distribution",
  xlab = "Estimated Difference"
)

abline(v = theta_hat, lty = 2)








The distribution appears roughly symmetric and centered near the estimate, supporting the normal approximation.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
To test whether the two CTAs perform differently, I test: H0​:πA​−πB​=0

se <- sqrt((pA_hat * (1 - pA_hat)) / n + 
           (pB_hat * (1 - pB_hat)) / n)

z_stat <- theta_hat / se
p_value <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z_stat)))

data.frame(
  Statistic = c("Estimate", "SE", "z", "p-value"),
  Value = c(theta_hat, se, z_stat, p_value)
)

  Statistic      Value
1  Estimate 0.02700000
2        SE 0.01786155
3         z 1.51162686
4   p-value 0.13062882


A small p-value suggests evidence against the null hypothesis.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
The A/B test can also be expressed as a regression problem.

group <- c(rep(1, n), rep(0, n))
outcome <- c(A, B)

model <- lm(outcome ~ group)
summary(model)


Call:
lm(formula = outcome ~ group)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-0.213 -0.213 -0.186 -0.186  0.814 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  0.18600    0.01264  14.719   <2e-16 ***
group        0.02700    0.01787   1.511    0.131    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.3996 on 1998 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.001141,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.0006413 
F-statistic: 2.283 on 1 and 1998 DF,  p-value: 0.131


The coefficient on the group variable equals the difference in means.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
In practice, repeatedly checking results during an experiment can lead to misleading conclusions. This is known as peeking.
Peeking increases the chance of finding a false positive because the test is effectively repeated multiple times. Proper experimental design requires fixing the sample size in advance or using methods that account for sequential testing.
